title: Export Model
selectProject: Select a project with a trained model to export
noModel: No trained model found. Complete training first.

ollama.title: Export to Ollama
ollama.hint: Ollama is a minimal tool for running large models locally. Once packed and imported into Ollama with one click, you can easily chat with your custom model directly in the terminal or with various client software! If you haven't installed Ollama, simply download it from ollama.com.
ollama.description: Convert your fine-tuned LoRA adapters into a standalone Ollama model for easy local use.
ollama.modelNameHint: Name for the model in Ollama (e.g. my-custom-model)
ollama.baseModelHint: Base model path or HuggingFace ID used during training
ollama.adapterHint: Select the training adapter to export, base model will be auto-filled
ollama.noAdapter: No trained adapter found. Complete training first.
ollama.quantizationHint: Quantization is the process of 'compressing' a large model. It significantly reduces the model's file size with minimal loss in intelligence, thereby lowering memory/VRAM requirements and speeding up execution. 4-bit offers maximum compression and speed; 8-bit is a balanced choice. If unsure, just stick with the default 4-bit.
ollama.successRunHint: Open your terminal and run the following command:
ollama.notInstalled: Ollama is not installed. Install from ollama.com first.

ollamaPathWarning: Configured Ollama models path '{{configuredPath}}' is missing the standard layout (manifests/registry.ollama.ai/library). Falling back to default path: {{fallbackPath}}
pathWarning: Configured export path '{{configuredPath}}' is unavailable (drive not mounted?). Files saved to local project directory: {{fallbackPath}}

gguf.title: Export as GGUF
gguf.description: Export as a .gguf file for use with llama.cpp, LM Studio, Jan, and other tools.
gguf.archNote: Supports Llama, Mistral, and Mixtral architectures only.

section.selectAdapterDesc: An adapter is the fine-tuned weight file generated after training — it contains what the model learned.
section.selectAdapterHint: Choose the adapter file you just trained. Think of this as a special skill pack added to the base model, letting it demonstrate the new abilities you taught it during the training phase.
section.modelNameDesc: After export, use this name to run your model in Ollama (e.g. ollama run your-name).
section.modelNameHint: Give your new model a great name! This name will be used to identify your model in Ollama or other tools later. It's recommended to include the original model name and your fine-tuning task, e.g., 'Qwen-MyTask-v1'.
section.quantizationDesc: Quantization controls model file size and speed. Higher precision means better quality but more memory.
section.quantizationHint: Quantization is the process of 'compressing' a large model. It significantly reduces the model's file size with minimal loss in intelligence, thereby lowering memory/VRAM requirements and speeding up execution. 4-bit offers maximum compression and speed; 8-bit is a balanced choice. If unsure, just stick with the default 4-bit.
section.exportModelHint: Once you've completed the above configuration, you can export your model here with one click. Supports exporting to Ollama (most common), or choose GGUF/MLX formats below. After export, you can use your custom model in various tools.

validation.needModelName: Please enter a model name first
validation.needQuantization: Please select a quantization method first

compat.title: Pre-export Check
compat.allGood: All checks passed — ready to export

keepFused.label: Also keep MLX model (for LM Studio)
keepFused.hint: Preserves the fused model directory alongside the Ollama export

mlx.title: Export to LM Studio (MLX Format)
mlx.description: Export as an MLX safetensors model, loadable directly in LM Studio (best performance on Mac) or as a local API server.
mlx.lmstudioHint: This folder can be loaded directly in LM Studio (MLX format — native performance on Mac).

server.title: Local Inference Server
server.description: Start an OpenAI-compatible API server from the exported MLX model. Any tool that supports the OpenAI API can connect.
server.noModel: Export an MLX model first to start the server.

guide.title: How to Use
guide.mlxSteps:
1. Open LM Studio → Load Model → Browse
2. Select the exported directory
3. Start chatting — native MLX performance on Mac
guide.serverSteps:
1. Click "Start Server" above
2. Connect any OpenAI-compatible tool:
   Base URL: {{endpoint}}
3. Works with: OpenWebUI, AnythingLLM, Chatbox, curl, etc.
guide.ggufSteps:
1. LM Studio: drag the .gguf file into the models folder
2. Jan: Import Model → select the .gguf file
3. llama.cpp: ./llama-cli -m <path_to_gguf>
